text: BARREIL (EL), or BARREL ISLET, a small island in the Old Bahama channel, to the N of Co. Romano, in N lat. 22° 25', W long. 77° 55'.

BARREIROS, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, which takes its rise in the Sierra, and running NE, SE, and E, unites, after the confluence of several other streams, with the Rio Grande on the E confines of the prov. in S lat. 16° 53'.

BARR-EL-MALHA, or BIR-EL-MALHA, a village in the Libyan desert, in the kingdom and 180 m. SW of New Dongola, on the route of the Sudan and Cairo caravan. It possesses good water, and the environs admit of cultivation.

BARREME, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Basses Alpes, arrond. of Digne. The cant. comprises 8 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 3,921. The town is situated near the l. bank of the Asse, 15 m. SW of Digne. Pop. 997.

BARREN, a county of Kentucky, U.S. It is watered by the Little Barren river, and the Beaver, an affluent of the Big Barren river. Cap., Glasgow. Pop. in 1830, 15,079; in 1840, 17,288; of whom 13,147 were whites, 4,065 slaves, and 76 free-coloured. The Big and Little Barren rivers fall into the Green river, the former in the NW of Warren co., the latter to the N of Greensburg.

BARREN CAPE, an island of the S Pacific, in the Furneaux group, at the E entrance of Bass' straits, in S lat. 40° 27', E long. 148° 17'. It is 20 m. in length, and 10 m. in average breadth, and, from its altitude, visible at a distance of from 30 to 40 m. At its eastern extremity is Barren-head, a low promontory stretching 2 m. outward, and fronted by two rocky islets. BAR
BARREN CREEK, a river of the United States, which takes its rise in the SW angle of Delaware, and falls into the Nanticote river in Somerset co., Maryland, 3 m. S of Vienna.

BARREN ISLAND, a small island in the Chinese sea, E of Singapore, in N lat. 1° 30', E long. 106° 30'. -Also an island in the S Pacific, at the W entrance of Bass' straits, the largest of Hunter's group, in S lat. 40° 32′, E long. 144° 48′.—Also a volcanic island in the gulf of Bengal, in N lat. 12° 15', and 66 m. E of Great Andaman island. It is about 18 m. in circumference, and its cone rises to the height of 1,800 ft. It is in a constant state of igneous action, and in its more violent eruptions sometimes discharges stones of 3 or 4 tons weight. With the exception of a few meagre patches of vegetation, the island is completely sterile.

BARREN ISLANDS, a group in the N Pacific, at the entrance of Cook's Inlet, on the southern coast of Russian America, in N lat. 57° 50'.—Also a group in the Mozambique channel, about 10 m. from the coast of Madagascar, in S lat. 18° 30'.
BARREN JOIE, or TENTH ISLAND, an island in the S Pacific, in Bass' straits, to the N of Stony-head, on the N coast of Van Diemen's Land, in N lat. 40° 56', E long. 147°.
BARREN PLAINS, a district of New South Wales, to the N of the Darling.
BARRENOI (CAPE), a mass of rocks projecting into the sea, on the coast of Siberia, between Cape Shelotskoi and the mouth of the Kovyma.
BARRERAS CAPE, or BEACHY-HEAD, a headland of Patagonia, on the E coast, in S lat. 49° 50', W long. 67° 48'.
BARRES-DE-NAINTRE (LES), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Vienne, and cant. of Chatellerault. Pop. 60.
BARRET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, and cant. of Barbezieux. Pop. 1,286.
BARRET'S GRANGE, a parish in co. Tipperary, in the barony of Middlethird. Area 1,056 acres. Pop. 424.
BARRHEAD, a manufacturing town in the p. of Neilston, in Renfrewshire, 3 m. SE of Paisley. Pop. 5,337.
BARRI, a town of Sudan, in the territory of Kanem, on the E coast of Lake Tchad, 140 m. NE of Kouka.

BARRIE, or BARRY, a parish in Forfarshire, stretching along the N shore of the frith of Tay, and terminating on the SE in the seaward promontory of Button-ness. Pop. 2,124. It comprises the villages of Carnoustie, Gardenbury, and Barrie.
BARRIER ISLAND. See AOTEA.
BARRIER ISLAND (LITTLE), an island off the E coast of the N island of New Zealand, in the gulf of Hauraki, about 9 m. from the main. It is uninhabited.

BARRIER REEF (GREAT), a gigantic coral reef stretching upwards of 1,200 m. along the NE coast of Australia, from Sandy cape, in S lat. 24° 30′, 600 m. N of Sydney, to Torres strait and the shores of New Guinea, in a direction nearly parallel to the line of coast. It forms a continuous submarine buttress or curtain, being broken only by narrow channels or gaps, towards its S extremity. The outer or ocean side rises precipitously from a great depth; the N extremity rests on the shoaler ground of Torres strait, and towards the S it rests on the sandy bank called Break-sea spit, running out from Sandy cape. "If it were to be laid dry, this great barrier would be found to have a considerable resemblance to a gigantic and irregular fortification,— a steep glacis crowned with a broken parapet-wall, and carried from one rising ground to another. The tower-like bastions of projecting and detached reefs would increase this resemblance. From examination of our charts it would appear that the normal condition of this long mass of reefs is that the outer barrier should be narrow, rising precipitously from a great depth, and running more or less nearly in a straight line; and that inside this outer barrier there should be a clear space about 20 fath. deep, and several miles wide, between which space and the land should be another body of reefs." The channel on the inner or W side of this great reef has an average width of about 30 m., with a navigable passage of from 10 to 30 fath. in depth, thoroughly sheltered from the ocean, and affording good anchorage at all points. The breadth of the Great B. reef near the surface appears to vary from 200 yards to a mile.-Jukes' Narrative of the Voyage of the Fly, 2 vols. 8vo. 1847. - Flinders' Voyage. BARRIGA NEGRA; a settlement and river in the Banda Oriental. The river rises about 130 m. to the N of Monte Video, in a small stream called Polancos, and flows into the Lagan Meni. It bears different names in different parts of its course, as the Godor, and the Zebolyati.—The settlement is 160 m. NE of Monte Video, and 120 m. from Maldonado, in a mountainous well-watered country, which is divided into great breeding estates, each stocked with from 60,000 to 200,000 head of cattle.
BARRIMA. See BAREMA.

BARRINGTON, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 6 m. SW of Cambridge. Area 2,129 acres. Pop. 533.—Also a p. in Somersetshire, 3 m. NE of Ilminster. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 531.—Also two parishes in Gloucestershire, distinguished as GREAT and LITTLE B., the former, 4 m. NW of Burford, having an area of 2,580 acres, and a pop. of 553; and the latter, 3 m. NW of Burford, with an area of 1,780 acres, and a pop. of 208.

BARRINGTON, a township in Strafford co., New Hampshire, U. S., 20 m. NW of Portsmouth. Pop. 1,844. There is a curious cave, called the Devil's den, in this township.—Also a township in Bristol co., Rhode island, U. S., 8 m. ESE of Providence. Pop. 549.—Also a township in Yates co., New York, U. S., 8 m. S of Penn Yan. Pop. 1,868.—Also a harbour and township of Nova Scotia, within Cape Sable island. The pop. are chiefly engaged in codfishing, and partially in grazing and agriculture.
BARRIOS (Los), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Andalusia, 12 m. NW of Gibraltar, on the road to Sanona. Pop. 2,000.

BARROSA, a tower, and vigia or watch-house, on the coast of Spain, in Andalusia, 12 m. from Conil. On the 5th of March, 1811, the French, under Victor, were defeated here by a small force under Graham, who had landed at Tarifa with the view of raising the siege of Cadiz. The expedition consisted of 11,200 Spaniards, 4,300 English and Portuguese, and 800 cavalry. Unfortunately the British general ceded the command to the Spanish general La Pena, who skulked away with the entire body of Spanish troops on the approach of Victor's brigades, leaving the British force, already exhausted by a long march, to encounter their onset unsupported. The French columns were, however, swept from the field with the bayonet. The engagement is recorded by Spahish annalists as a brilliant victory obtained by La Pena; while French historians, with equal gravity and truth, record how Graham's triple line of 3,000 men each were culbute by Victor!

BARROW, a large and important river of Ireland, in the prov. of Leinster. Its name is a corruption of Barragh, which designates it as 'the boundary river,' and was given it in consequence of its being for many years the boundary between the BAR English pale and the Irish septs. Its ancient name was variously Birga, Breva, and Breba. The river rises at the head of Glenbarrow, in the Slievebloom mountains, on the western border of Queen's co. Its direction over about 17 m., to the vicinity of Monastereven, is prevailingly eastward; and thence to the head of Waterford harbour, or the influx of the Suir, a distance in a straight line of about 65 m., it is prevailingly southward. Its chief tributaries are the Ownass, a little NE of Mountmellick; the Little Barrow, in the vicinity of Monastereven; the Burren, at Carlow; the Nore, 21 m. above New Ross; and the Suir, at the head of Waterford harbour. The principal towns upon its banks are Portarlington, Monastereven, Athy, Carlow, Leighlin Bridge, Gore's Bridge, and New Ross. The current of the river, in the upland or early part of its course, is rapid, and along a rugged path. The subsequent and larger section of its course is slow, majestic, and sinuous, through a country generally level. Previous to 1811, nearly £42,000 had been expended in improving the navigation of the B.; and, up to 1838, the sum expended was £177,852. From the sea to New Ross, the river is navigable by vessels of 200 tons; thence to Athy, it is navigable by barges; and from Athy, it sends off a still-water navigation to join the Grand canal, and communicate with Dublin and a great portion of central Ireland. A chief obstruction to deep navigation above New Ross, is a considerable bar below the influx of the Nore. The navigation upward from that point is chiefly in the natural bed of the river; but is conveyed past obstruction by means of short canal cuts, and 17 locks; and is aided, over the whole distance, by a horse trackway.

BARROW (LITTLE), a river of Leinster. It rises in the bog of Allen; flows SW past Rathangan, and falls into the Barrow, half-a-mile above Monastereven. Its chief tributary, the Feagile, is about equal to it in both length and volume, and joins it about <math>2\frac{3}{4}</math> m. above the confluence with the B.

BARROW, a parish in Cheshire, 5 m. ENE of Chester, comprising the two townships of Great and Little Barrow. Area 2,620 acres. Pop. 668.—Also a township in the p. of Holystone in Northumberland.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Cottesmore, in Rutlandshire, 6 m. N by E of Oakham. Pop. 142. -Also a parish in the co. of Salop, 4 m. E by S of Much Wenlock. Area 2,290 acres. Pop. 383.- Also a parish in Suffolk, 6 m. W of Bury St. Edmunds. Area 2,810 acres. Pop. 995.
BARROW (North and South), two adjoining parishes in Somersetshire, 5 m. SW of Castle Carey, Area of N. B. 970 acres. Pop. 140. Area of S. B. 580 acres. Pop. 140.
BARROW'S BAY, a small gulf on the E coast of Loo Choo island, in N lat. 26° 26'.

BARROW (CAPE), a headland on the coast of the Arctic ocean, in N lat. 67° 54', W long. 110° 42', forming the E side of Detention harbour. The coast here consists of steep craggy granite mountains, rising to 1,400 or 1,500 ft., and so abruptly from the water's edge as to admit of few landing-places even for a canoe.—Also a point on the coast of Fernando Po island, in N lat. 3° 111, E long. 8° 40'.
BARROW-GURNEY, a parish in Somersetshire, 5 m. SW of Bristol. Area 2,130 acres. Pop. 303.

BARROW ISLAND, an island in the S Pacific, in 20° 45′ 07" S lat., and 4° 07' 48" W of Gambier island. It is a narrow ring of land, of an oval form, not more than 200 wide in any part, clothed with a dense vegetation and enclosing a lagune filled with shoals of fish. Its only inhabitants, when discovered by Beechey in 1826, were a few black and white tern; tropic, and frigate birds.

BARROW'S ISLAND, an island off the W coast of Australia, in S lat. 20° 35', about 12 m. broad, and 20 m. in length. Its N and E coasts are composed of red sandstone cliffy projections, separated by sandy bays, and fronted by a coral reef. The soil is thin and scanty, nourishing the spinifex and some stunted brushwood. It appears to be uninhabited.—Stokes.

BARROW (Mount), a conspicuous hill, about 300 ft. in alt., on the W coast of Coronation gulf, in Arctic America, in N lat, 68° 20', and has a flat summit bounded by limestone cliffs. [Franklin.]- Also one of the Peel range in New South Wales, in S lat. 33° 47'.

BARROW (Point), a low tongue of land, forming the most northern point on the coast of Arctic America, 126 m. NE of Icy cape, in N lat. 71° 23' 31", W long. 156° 21′ 30". The land slopes gradually from Cape Smyth, where it is about 45 ft. in height, to this point. The depth about half-a-mile from the cape is between 6 and 7 fath., with the current setting NE.—Beechey.

BARROW'S STRAITS, a channel leading from the NW part of Baffin's bay into the Polar sea, along the parallel of 74° N. It is about 250 m. in length, and from 30 m. to 45 m. in breadth. The shores on both sides are steep and cliffy, and 75 fath. is the least depth which has yet been sounded. Captain Parry discovered these straits, or was the first to sail through them in 1819-20. They are now frequented by whaling-ships.

BARROW-UPON-HUMBER, a parish in Lincolnshire, 8 m. N of Glandford Brigg. Area 4,620 acres. Pop. 1,662.—Also two parishes adjoining, and distinguished as B. St. Mary, and B. St. Peter; the united area of which is 6,710 acres. Pop. of B. St. Mary, 1,842; of B. St. Peter, 1,633.
BARROW-UPON-SOAR, a parish in Leicestershire, 2 m. N of Mount Sorrel. Area 7,950 acres. Pop. 5,782.
BARROW-UPON-TRENT, a parish in Derbyshire, 6 m. S of Derby. Area 4,040 acres. Pop. 641.
BARROWBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 2 m. W of Grantham. Area 4,010 acres. Pop. 799.
BARROWDEN, a parish in Rutlandshire, 6 m. E of Uppingham. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. 658.
BARROWFORD, a township in the parish of Whalley, Lancashire. Area 1,540 acres. Pop. 2,630.
BARRY, a parish in Glamorganshire, 9 m. SW of Cardiff. Pop., including Barry isle in the Bristol channel, 104.

BARRY, a county in the W part of Michigan, U.S., on the Thorn-apple river. Area 576 sq. m. Pop. 1,078. The cap. is Hastings.—Also a county in Missouri, U. S., in the SW part of the state. Area 830 sq. m. Pop. 4,795.—Also a township in Schuylkill co., in Pennsylvania, U. S., on Deep creek, a tributary of the Susquehanna. Pop. 639.

BARS, or Barsch, a comitat or administrative province of Hungary; bounded on the N by the com. of Neutra and Thurocz; on the E by those of Sohl and Honth; on the S by those of Gran and Komorn; and on the W by Neutra. Its length is 44 m.; breadth, 27 m.; and it comprises a superficies of 1,000 sq. m., with a pop. in 1837, of 133,600, consisting of Slavonians—who form the majority of the number-Hungarians, and Germans. This prov. presents a generally hilly surface, especially towards the N; and is intersected in a SSE direction by the Gran, which here takes its course through a series of valleys of great luxuriance. About one-third of the entire surface is under cultivation; and a somewhat larger proportion is covered with forests. The principal productions of the soil, besides timber, are grain and wine in the northern districts, and, in the southern, lint. Cattle are reared in considerable numbers. BAR The formerly rich mines of gold and silver, in the igneous mountains of Uj-Banya, are now nearly exhausted. Mill-stone of good quality is abundant in that locality, and also in the valley of Shano, and forms an important article of export to Slavonia. Tanneries and cloth manufactories constitute the staple of the manufacturing industry of the prov.—The com. of B. is divided into 4 jaras or marches, viz., Oszlan, Tapoltsany, Verebely, and Leva; and comprises 2 free royal towns, viz., Königsberg the capital, and Kremnitz; 11 towns, and 199 villages. Its contribution to the military treasury, or war-department of the kingdom of Hungary, amounts to 95,533 florins, or about £81,000, of which 10,568 florins is contributed by the 2 royal towns.

BARS, or Bremsenburg, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of the same name, 12 m. SSW of Königsberg. It is divided by the Gran into two parts, of which that on the l. is named O-Bars; and the opposite, Uj-Bars. The latter contains a strong castle, from which the prov. derived its name, and was formerly a free royal town. It has much declined in importance. O-Bars is still flourishing and populous.

BARSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, and cant. of Podensac, on the l. bank of the Garonne, 22 m. SE of Bordeaux. It is celebrated for its white wines; and has quarries of free and paving stones. Pop. 2,896.
BARSANSANG, a town on the Upper Gambia, near the SE end of M'Carthy's island.
BARSE, a river of France, in the dep. of Aube, which flows into the Seine at Troyes. Its course is 25 m. in length, of which 15 are navigable by boats of small burden.

BARSHAM, a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. W of Beccles. Area 1,470 acres. Pop. 250.—Also three parishes in the hund. of Gallow in Norfolk, distinguished as East, North, and West B., with areas respectively of 1,230, 710, and 1,380 acres; and pop. of 240, 89, and 86.
BARSHURI, a small Beluchi town, in Kutch Gundava, 29 m. NNW of Rajhan, on the road to Mirpur.
BARSINGHAUSEN, a village of Hanover, in the circle of Kalenberg, SW of Hanover. Pop. 330. There is a Lutheran convent of noble ladies here.
BARSOE, a small island of Denmark, in the Little Belt, in the duchy of Sleswig, and bail. of Hadersleben, in N lat. 55° 7'.
BARSTABLE, a hundred in Essex. Area 73,290 acres. Pop. in 1831, 13,181; in 1841, 15,044.
BARSTON, a parish in Warwickshire, 11 m. NNW of Warwick. Area 1,990 acres. Pop. 392.
BART, a township of Pennsylvania, U. S., in Lancaster co., 55 m. E of Harrisburg. It is intersected by a head-stream of the Octarara river. Pop. 1,859.
BARTABAT, a town of Central Egypt, on the Bahr-Yusuf, 40 m. SW, of Benisuef.

BARTAN, a river of Anatolia, in the sanjak of Boli, which rises on the N flank of the Durna Jailassidagh, and flowing NW, pierces the Itchiller-dagh in a course from E to W, and then bends NNW, passing Durimasi and Murad Bei, and the town of Bartan at the point where it receives the Kodshanas-Su on its l. bank, and falls into the Euxine a few miles SW of Amiserah. It is identified with the ancient Parthenius.—The town of B., situated 6 m. from the mouth of the river-which has 4 ft. of water to the sea-is a place of some trade, exporting wax, boxwood, and fruits. Boats of from 60 to 100 tons are built here. The country in the vicinity is low and swampy.
BARTCHUK, a town of Tibet, on the l. bank of the Kashgar, 80 m. NE of Yarkund.

BARTEN, or BARTHEN, a town of Prussia, in the circle of Königsberg, 10 m. N of Rastenberg. It is well-built, and is defended by a fort which was formerly the residence of the bishop of Pomerania. It contains some cloth manufactories and tanneries. Pop. in 1837, 1,533.
BARTENHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Rhin, cant. of Landser. Pop. 1,560.

BARTENSTEIN, a town of Wurtemberg, of considerable antiquity, in the circle of Jaxt, near the r. bank of the Ette, 8 m. NNW of Gerabroun. It is situated on the acclivity of a mountain, and contains a fine castle, the former residence of the princes of Hohenlohe-Waldenburg-Bartenstein. Pop. 1,082.

BARTENSTEIN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of E Prussia, and circle of Friedland, on the r. bank of the Alle, 33 m. SSE of Königsberg. It was founded in 1332, and contains 3 churches and a college. It possesses manufactories of cloth, linen, tanneries, and potteries. Pop. in 1837, 4,111.—Also a town of Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle. Pop. 1,050.
BARTER ISLE, a small island in the Arctic ocean, near the coast of Russian America, in N lat. 70° 15′, W long. 144°.

BARTFA, or BARTFELD, a town of Hungary, in the circle of Saros, 20 m. N of Eperies, on the Tepla river. Pop. 5,000. The trade of the town consists in wines, linen, cloth, and yarn. The baths of Tepla and Lauka, in the vicinity, are the most celebrated in Hungary.

BARTH, a seaport of Prussia, in Pomerania, on a small river of the same name, which rises near Stralsund, and flows into the Barther Bodden, a small gulf of the Baltic, to the NW of Franzburg, in N lat. 54° 23′. Pop. 3,880.
BARTHE-DE-NESTE (LA), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees. The com. is 10 m. E of Bagneres-en-Bigorre. Pop. 732.—The cant. comprises 21 com.

BARTHE'LEMY (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Seyches, 9 m. E of Marmande. Pop. 1,422.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Angers. Pop. 1,074.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Manche, cant. and 2 m. N of Mortain. Pop. 600.
BARTHELE'MY-DE-GROAIN (SAINT), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Isère, com. of La Gua, 12 m. SSW of Grenoble. Pop. 150. There is a curious spring here, whence arises an inflammable gas.
BARTHELEMY-LESTRA (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Feurs. Pop. 842.

BARTHELEMY-LE-PIN (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of La Mastre. Pop. 214. BARTHELEMY-DE-SECHILIENNE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Vizille. Pop. 1,000.
BARTHELEMY-DE-VALS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Drome, cant. of Saint-Vallier. Pop. 1,043.
BARTHOLOMA (SANCT), a town of Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, 8 m. ESE of Gmund. Pop. 868.
BARTHOLOMAUS-SEE, or König-See, a mountain lake in the SE of Bavaria, 4 m. from Berchtesgaden. It is surrounded by dark lofty mountains, and receives the waters of the Königsbach.
BARTHOLOMEW, a county in Jowa, U.S., near the centre of the state. Area 480 sq. m. Pop. 10,042.
BARTHOLOMEW (CAPE), the southernmost point of Staten-Land, in Le Maire straits, at the extremity of South America.

BARTHOLOMEW (SAINT), one of the Caribbee islands, in the West Indies, in N lat. 17° 50', W long. BAR elevation, and about 25 m. in circumference; and is fertile in sugar, cotton, tobacco, cassava, and indigo. Its trees of the highest estimation are the soap or aloe, the caleback, the canapia from which is extracted a gum of excellent cathartic qualities, and the parotane, whose branches growing downwards take root again, and grow upwards, forming an impenetrable barrier. All along the shore are trees called seatrees, whose boughs are curiously plaited together, and look as if they were glazed. Lignumvitæ and iron wood trees abound. The geological formation is confusedly stratified. The rocks chiefly consist of limestone. The shores of this island are dangerous, and cannot be safely approached without a good pilot; but it has an excellent harbour on its W side, called Le Carenage, in which ships of any size are sheltered from all winds. The pop. is about 8,000. Half of its inhabitants are Irish Roman Catholics, whose predecessors settled here in 1666; the others are of French descent. This island was ceded by France to Sweden in 1785. Its only town is called Gustavia.
BARTHOLOMEW (SAINT), an island on the NE point of Bougainville's strait, in S lat. 15° 48', 8 m. from Mallicolo. It is about 20 m. in circumf.; is well-wooded, and inhabited.
BARTHOLOMEW (SAINT), the name of several town and city-parishes in England.
BARTHOMLEY, a parish partly in Cheshire, and partly in Staffordshire, 6 m. S by E of Sandbach. Area 11,600 acres. Pop. 3,609.
BARTIN. See BARTAN.
BARTIN, a village of Prussia, in Pomerania, on the Busternitz, N of Rummelsburg. Pop. 222.
BARTLETT, a township in Coos co., New Hampshire, U. S., 80 m. NNE of Concord, to the E of the White mountains, on the Saco river. Pop. 706.— Also a township in Jefferson co., Pennsylvania.
BARTLOF (Gross), a town of Prussian Saxony, in the circle of Erfurt, SE of Heiligenstadt. Pop. 1,000. Woollen stuffs and serge are manufactured here.
BARTLOW, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 2 m. E by S of Linton. Area 320 acres. Pop. 89.
BARTLOW-END, or LITTLE BARTLOW, a hamlet in the p. of Ashdon, 5 m. NE of Saffron-Walden. Pop. 216.
BARTODZIEIE, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Sandomir, circle and 14 m. from Radom. Pop. 560.
BARTOLOME' DE TORMES (SAN), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Avila, partido of Piedrahita, on the Ribera, an affluent of the Tormes.
BARTOLOME' (SANTO), a small river in the W part of Sicily, flowing SW and then NNW into the bottom of the gulf of Castel a Mare.
BARTOLOME' DE LOS LLANOS (SAN), a district and large village of Mexico, in the prov. of Chiapa.

BARTOLOME' (SAN), a small port on the coast of Old California, in N lat. 27° 40', W long. 115° 10'. -Also a town of New Granada, on the l. bank of the Magdalena, at the confluence of a river of the same name, 125 m. E of Antioquia.—Also a town of Ecuador, on the r. bank of the Napo, 10 m. SSE of San Miguel.

BARTOLOMEO (Santo), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, 6 m. S of Volturara. Pop. 4,600.—Also a town of Lombardy, in the district of Verona, S of Legnago.—Also a village in the Swiss canton of Ticino, 5 m. NW of Bellinzona.

BARTON, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 31 m. SW of Cambridge. Area 1,500 acres. Pop. 319.—Also a township in Farndon p., Cheshire, 81 m. S of Chester. Pop. 169.—Also a township in Preston p., Lancashire, 51 m. N of Preston. Area 1,510 acres. 62° 50′, 30 m. N of St. Christopher's. It is of slight | Pop. 413.—Also a p. in Nottinghamshire, 5 m. SW of Nottingham. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 333.- Also a hamlet in Bedford p., Warwickshire. Pop. 147.—Also a p. in Westmoreland, 13 m. W by N of Appleby. Area 33,690 acres. Pop. 1,668, with the chapelries of Martindale and Patterdale, and the townships of High Barton, Sockbridge, Low Winder, and Eamont-bridge. - Also a p. in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. SW of Darlington. Area 2,790 acres. Pop. 631, of whom 567 were in the township of B.

BARTON, a township in Orleans co., in the state of Vermont, 42 m. NE of Montpelier, on a river of the same name which flows N into Lake Memphremagog. Pop. 892.—Also a township in Twya co., New York, 174 m. SW of Albany, on the Susquehanna. Pop. 2,324.
BARTON-IN-THE-BEANS, a township lying in several parishes, but returned in that of Mailstone, in Leicestershire, 2 m. N of Market-Bosworth.
BARTON-BENDISH, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. N of Stokeferry. Area 4,390 acres. Pop. 455.
BARTON-BLOUNT, a parish in Derbyshire, 11 m. W of Derby. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. 68.
BARTON-IN-THE-CLAY, a parish in Bedfordshire, 4 m. S of Silsoe. Area 2,270 acres. Pop. 855.
BARTON-ST.-DAVID, a parish in Somersetshire, 5 m. NE of Somerton. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 455.
BARTON (GREAT), a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. NE of Bury St. Edmunds. Area 4,030 acres. Pop. 774.
BARTON-HARTSHORN, a parish in Bucks, 4 m. SW of Buckingham. Area 870 acres. Pop. 165.
BARTON-ON-THE-HEATH, a parish in Warwickshire, 5 m. S of Shipston-on-Stour. Area 1,540 acres. Pop. 212.

BARTON-UPON-HUMBER, a parish and market-town in the N division of the wapentake of Yarborough, Lincolnshire; 166 m. N of London, 32 m. NE of Lincoln, and 7 m. S of Hull. The town takes its name from its situation on the S bank of the Humber, on a rising ground at the extremity of the wolds, about three quarters of a mile from the river. It was a place of considerable commerce in the time of the Conqueror; but suffered much when Edward I. made Kingston-upon-Hull a free borough. It now derives its chief importance from the ferry over the Humber—here upwards of 6 m. broad—to Kingston-upon-Hull.—The town consists of two spacious, but irregularly-built streets in which there are some good modern houses. The principal trade is in corn and flour; and a considerable number of the inhabitants are employed in the manufacture of sacking, ropes, bricks, and tiles. B. is a pollingplace for the member for Lindsey. The manor belongs to the Crown. The town is within the jurisdiction of the county-magistrates. A large cattlemarket is held here every alternate Monday; and a fair for cattle on Trinity-Thursday. Pop., in 1801, 1,709; in 1831, 3,231; in 1841, 3,475. The p. has an area of 6,710 acres.
BARTON-UPON-IRWELL, a township in the p. of Eccles, in Lancashire, 6 m. W of Manchester. Area 10,530 acres. Pop. 10,865.
BARTON-ST.-MARY'S, a hamlet in the p. of St. Mary de Lode, in Gloucestershire. Area, with Tuffley, 1,470 acres. Pop. 1,781.
BARTON-ST.-MICHAELS, a hamlet in the p. of St. Michael, in Gloucestershire. Area 500 acres. Pop. 1,116.
BARTON-MILLS, a parish in Suffolk, in the hund. of Lockford. Area 2,050 acres. Pop. 640.
BARTON-UNDER-NEEDWOOD, a chapelry in the p. of Tatenhill, Staffordshire, 5 m. SW of Burton - upon - Trent. Area 3,520 acres. Pop. 1,459.
BARTON-SEAGRAVE, a parish in Northamp- BAR tonshire, 2 m. SE of Kettering. Area 2,030 acres. Pop. 219.
BARTON-STACEY, a hundred and parish in Hants, 5 m. SW of Whitchurch. Area 4,520 acres Pop. 561, of whom 346 were in the tything of the same name.
BARTON-STEEPLE, a parish in Oxfordshire, 5 m. SW of Doddington. Area 2,710 acres. Pop., with the three townships of B. Middle, B. Sesswells, and B. Steeple, 640.
BARTON-LE-STREET, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NW of New Malton. Area 3,180 acres. Pop. 419.
BARTON-TURF, a parish in Norfolk, 6 m. SE of North Walsham. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 408.
BARTON-WESTCOTT, a parish in Oxfordshire, 3 m. E of Enstone. Area 650 acres. Pop. 290.
BARTON-LE-WILLOWS, a township in Crambe p., in the N. R. of Yorkshire. Area 980 acres. Pop. 207.
BARTONICO (SAN), a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Chihuahua, near the frontiers of Durango.
BARTRA, or BARTRACH, an island in the p. of Killalla, in co. Mayo, near the head of Killalla bay, 3 m. from the W coast. It is about 21 m. in length, and nowhere quite a 1 m. in breadth.

BARTSCH, a river of Prussia, rising in the S part of the prov. of Posen, between Mixtat and Grabow, passing Adelnau, Militsch, and Hernnstadt, and flowing into the Oder, on the r. side, near Schwersen, after a course of nearly 100 m. It is navigable from Militsch.
BARU. See BAROU.
BARUGH, or BARGH, a township in the p. of Darton, W. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. NW of Barnsley. Area 1,660 acres. Pop. 1,266.
BARUGH-AMBO, a township in the p. of Kirkby Misperton, N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SW of Pickering. Area 2,150 acres. Pop. 150.
BARUMGULA, a town of Northern India, in the Punjab, in the Nandan Sar pass leading into Kashmir, at an alt. of 6,800 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 500.
BARUTH, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, 30 m. SE of Potsdam, on the Geile, an affluent of the Saar. Pop. in 1837, 1,605.—Also a town of Saxony, on the Lobau, 8 m. W by N of Bautzen. Pop. 438.
BARVAS, a parish of Ross-shire, on the island of Lewis, stretching 15 m. along the N coast. Pop. in 1801, 2,233; in 1841, 3,850, chiefly small cottars renting patches of ground.
BARWAH, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, 10 m. SSE of Jansi.

BARWALDE, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, in the circle of Konigsberg, 32 m. N of Frankfort, on a lake of the same name. Pop. in 1837, 2,817.—Also a town of Pomerania, in the circle of Köslin. Pop. 1,274.—Also a town of Silesia, on the Spree, in the circle of Hoyerswerda.
BARWARRAH, a fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Ajmeer, 10 m. W of Rantampur.
BARWELL, a parish in Leicestershire, 2 m. N of Hinckley. Area 3,950 acres. Pop., with the hamlets of Potter's-Marston and Stapleton, 1,607.
BARWICK, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. W of Burnham-Westgate. Area 890 acres. Pop. 32.— Also a p. in Somersetshire, 2 m. S of Yeovil. Area 920 acres. Pop., with Stoford hamlet, 446.
BARWICK-IN-ELMET, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. S of Wetherby. Area 8,030 acres. Pop., with the township of Roundhay, 2,275.

BARWON, or BARWURN, a river in the Port Philip district of Australia, which, descending from the Barabul hills, and flowing E, joins the Marabul running S, after which the united stream flows 3 m. SE to Geelong harbour. The exposed situation of its mouth renders it useless for water-carriage.—Stokes.
BARY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, 30 m. N by W of Lucknow.
BARYSZ, a town of Austria, in Galicia, in the circle of Lemberg, on the Barysza, 20 m. ENE of Stanislawow. Pop. 1,000.
BARZIL, a large village of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, and district of Mishkin, between Ahar and Ardabil. Wheat, barley, and rice are cultivated in the surrounding territory.
BAS. See BATZ.
BASACHIK. See BAZARJIK.
BASBOCH, or ATTARFAIEH, a town of Nubia, in Sennaar, on the r. bank of the Blue Nile, 10 m. N by E of Sennaar.
BASCARA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Gerona in Catalonia, on the r. bank of the Flavia, 8 m. NE of Gerona.

BAS-EN-BASSET, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire, arrond. of Yssengeaux.—The cant. comprises 6 com., with a pop. in 1831, of 12,536.—The com. is on the 1. bank of the Loire, 12 m. N of Yssengeaux. Pop. 5,524.
BASCHARAGE, a town of Luxemburg, 12 m. W by S of Luxemburg. Pop. 900.
BASCHI, a town of Italy, in the States-of-the- Church, 25 m. W of Spoleto, on the Tiber.
BASCHKIRS. See BASHKIRS.
BASCHLI. See BASHLI.
BASCHURCH, a parish in Salop, 7 m. NW of Shrewsbury. Area 10,270 acres. Pop. 1,491.
BASCONS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Grenade-sur-l'Adour. Pop. 1,150.
BASDAHL, a village of Hanover, 30 m. SW of Stade. Pop. 160.
BASECLES, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 16 m. E of Tournay. Pop. 2,245. There are extensive quarries of a fine calcareous stone here.
